titleStmt.title: Letter from the Advertising Manager of the Belfast Telegraph to Sir Matthew Nathan, 19 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Advertising Manager of the Belfast Telegraph

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from the Advertising Manager of the Belfast Telegraph to Sir Matthew Nathan. The advertising manager requests that the Belfast Telegraph be added to the list of newspapers that receiving the Belfast Police Courts advertisements, as it claims to have the largest circulation of any newspaper published in Ireland. The letter also encloses a newspaper advertisement concerning the election for the position of an assistant clerkship in the police courts Belfast.Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7109/1

correspAction.persName: Advertising Manager of the Belfast Telegraph

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Belfast, 19th April, 1916 The Under-Secretary, to His Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, Dublin Castle, DUBLIN. Dear Sir, I understand the system of advertising vacancies
in the Belfast Police Court has been arranged so that two
newspapers only receive these advertisments, namely, the
''Belfast News-Letter'',which represent the Unionist side. I claim that the 'Belfast Evening Telegraph' is
a newspaper which should be availed of to advertise all
Government appointments in Belfast. It has the largest
circulation of any newspaper published in Ireland, and as
that circulation is confined to Ulster, two-thirds of same
being in the city of Belfast and one-third through the
province, there is no newspaper which can equal pub-
licity. While I have no desire that either of the news-
papers mentioned should be deprived of these advertisements,
I think that the 'Belfast Telegraph' should be added. The Constabulary of Belfast have for many years
appealed to the 'Belfast Telegraph' for free publicity of
To The Under-Secretary
to His Excellency the Lord Lieutenant.
information they desire to convey to the public. In fact,
the 'Belfast Telegraph' is the only newspaper the Police
utilize in endeavoring to obtain information as to the
owners of lost property, persons missing and, when necessary,
the identification of dead bodies found, etc., etc. This
testimony to the value of the 'Belfast Telegraph' as a means
of reaching the public is my reason for suggesting that this
paper should be added to the list of newspapers receiving
these and other official advertisements. I am certain if you would address the Commissioner
of Police, or any official who has resided in Belfast, he
would corroborate the above statement. Yours truly, The Advertisement Manager
The Proprietors of the 'Belfast Evening Telegraph'
would feel obliged by an order to insert enclosed
Advertisement. APPOINTMENT
ASSISTANT CLERKSHIP,
POLICE COURTS, BELFAST.
HIS EXCELLENCY THE LORD LIEU-
TENANT has directed that an ELEC-
TION shall be held for an ASSISTANT
CLERKSHIP ( 3rd Class ) in the POLICE
COURTS, BELFAST. The ELECTION to fill
this Post will take place at the POLICE COURTS,
BELFAST, on WEDNESDAY, the 3rd day of
May, 1916, at the hour of Three o'clock p. m. The order of the Lord Lieutenant, made under
7 Edward VII., Cap. 22, directs that a Candi-
date for the Post of Assistant at Belfast shall for
the period of the War be between the ages of 41
and 50 years. Candidates will be required to have a know-
ledge of Typewriting and Shorthand, and should
present themselves to the Chief Clerk one week
before the Election with a view of this qualifica-
tion being tested. The Salary for the vacant Clerkship will com-
mence at £80 per annum, increasing to £150 per
annum by increments of £7 per annum. The Candidate elected will be examined by the
Medical Officer of the Registrar of Petty Ses-
sions Clerks' Department, and will be required
to satisfy the Registrar as to his educational
qualifications for the appointment.
( By Order )
( Signed ) JOHN G. BALLENTINE.,
Chief Clerk
862
18th April 1916.
